title: Lesson Quiz: Understanding Credit and Credit Scores
description: Assessment of the concept that How credit works and factors affecting credit scores.

choices.text: The ability to borrow money and repay it later
choices.feedback: Correct. Credit allows a person to borrow money that must later be repaid.

choices.text: Money that never has to be repaid
choices.feedback: Borrowed money must still be repaid.

choices.text: Only the money already saved in an account
choices.feedback: Credit is about borrowing, not simply saving.

choices.text: A fee charged on every purchase

questions.prompt: Making payments on time is usually stronger for credit than missing payments.

choices.feedback: On-time payments are usually stronger because they show responsible borrowing behavior.
Correct. Responsible repayment behavior is usually better for credit than missed payments.

questions.prompt: Why do credit scores matter in borrowing decisions?

choices.text: Because they guarantee every borrower will be approved
choices.feedback: Credit scores do not guarantee approval. They help estimate borrowing risk.

choices.text: Because they eliminate the need to repay debt
choices.feedback: A credit score does not remove repayment responsibility.

choices.text: Because they help estimate borrowing risk
choices.feedback: Correct. Credit scores are often used to judge how risky a borrower may be.

choices.text: Because they replace all financial planning
choices.feedback: Credit scores are one factor in borrowing decisions, not a replacement for planning.

questions.prompt: Which behavior is more likely to weaken future borrowing opportunities?

choices.feedback: On-time payment behavior is generally stronger for future borrowing.
Comparing choices is responsible behavior, not the one most likely to weaken future borrowing opportunities.
Comparing consequences is a stronger financial behavior.

choices.text: Missing payments and ignoring due dates
choices.feedback: Correct. Weak repayment behavior can damage future borrowing opportunities.

questions.prompt: Credit decisions should consider long-term consequences, not only short-term access to money.

choices.feedback: Correct. Strong credit decisions should consider future borrowing effects and repayment responsibility.
Credit decisions are stronger when they consider both current access and future consequences.
